coredata.dc:title: Isolating the effects of photon reabsorption in the photoluminescence spectrum of carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: C.M., Devan
M.M., Bijeesh
Sahoo, Subhamoy
Bhattacharyya, Jayeeta
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are highly emissive nanoparticles that exhibit broad photoluminescence (PL) across a wide frequency range in solutions and thin films. However, at high concentrations, CDs suffer from PL quenching, traditionally attributed to aggregation effects, which induce PL spectral shifts and changes in PL lifetimes. In this study, we demonstrate that photon reabsorption is a pivotal, often overlooked mechanism contributing to emission quenching, without any change in lifetimes. An excitation depth-dependent PL measurement was performed to separate out the effects of reabsorption in the emission spectrum of CDs. Photon reabsorption arises from the wavelength-dependent absorption of emitted photons as they propagate through the CD solution, leading to a redshift in the emission peak and a reduction in PL intensity. We corrected the apparent spectral shift by accounting for the absorption of the CD sample in the region where the emission and absorption spectra overlap. Furthermore, time-resolved PL decay measurements ruled out aggregation effects, confirming that reabsorption is the primary mechanism causing PL quenching in our sample. These findings highlight the necessity of addressing reabsorption effects to accurately measure the PL spectra for emissive species, such as CDs, which exhibit broad absorption and emission with significant spectral overlap.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
PL quenching
Reabsorption
Depth-dependent PL

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Concentration dependent PL quenching in CDs
Effect of reabsorption in the emission spectra of CDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the experimental setup to study reabsorption.

Absorption spectrum (dotted line) of CD solution (0.0066
C
0
) and PL spectrum (black line) for 390 nm excitation.
(a) Concentration dependent PL of CDs and (b) change in PL peak intensity with concentration, fitted with the expression given in (1).

Time resolved PL decay for CD solution at 0.0066
C
0
and 0.143
C
0
concentrations at (a) 445 nm and (b) 525 nm.

PL spectra for varying excitation depth for CD solutions at (a)
0 . 0066
C
0
and (b)
0 . 143
C
0
.
Change in emission intensity with varying depth at different emission wavelengths. Inset shows exponential decay fitting of the change in PL intensity at 450 nm.

Apparent redshift in the PL spectra has been reproduced by taking into account, the contribution from absorption tail. The redshifted spectrum at 7 mm depth (square) is replicated (open circle) by dividing the PL spectrum at 0 mm depth (solid circle) by the absorbance (dashed line).
Lifetime data for PL emission at 445 nm and 525 nm for CD solution at different concentrations.
Comparison of absorption coefficients obtained from fitting and experiment.

head.ce:title: Isolating the effects of photon reabsorption in the photoluminescence spectrum of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Madras, Chennai, 600036, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Madras

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Madras, Chennai, 600036, Tamilnadu, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Presently at Nanyang Technological University, Singapore.
Presently at University of Würzburg, Germany.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are highly emissive nanoparticles that exhibit broad photoluminescence (PL) across a wide frequency range in solutions and thin films. However, at high concentrations, CDs suffer from PL quenching, traditionally attributed to aggregation effects, which induce PL spectral shifts and changes in PL lifetimes. In this study, we demonstrate that photon reabsorption is a pivotal, often overlooked mechanism contributing to emission quenching, without any change in lifetimes. An excitation depth-dependent PL measurement was performed to separate out the effects of reabsorption in the emission spectrum of CDs. Photon reabsorption arises from the wavelength-dependent absorption of emitted photons as they propagate through the CD solution, leading to a redshift in the emission peak and a reduction in PL intensity. We corrected the apparent spectral shift by accounting for the absorption of the CD sample in the region where the emission and absorption spectra overlap. Furthermore, time-resolved PL decay measurements ruled out aggregation effects, confirming that reabsorption is the primary mechanism causing PL quenching in our sample. These findings highlight the necessity of addressing reabsorption effects to accurately measure the PL spectra for emissive species, such as CDs, which exhibit broad absorption and emission with significant spectral overlap.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Carbon dots (CDs) are luminescent nanoparticles consisting of an sp
2
-hybridized carbon core with various functional groups on their surfaces [1]. Their optical properties are mainly governed by these surface states in the visible wavelength range, while the weaker core emission remains largely confined to the UV region [2]. Surface-state emissions in CDs exhibit desirable characteristics such as high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), tunable emission wavelengths, and commonly show excitation wavelength-dependent emission [3–5]. These attributes make CDs a suitable choice for imaging and optoelectronic applications [6,7]. CDs also stand out for their simple, cost-effective synthesis, low toxicity, and bio-compatibility, making them ideal for bio-imaging applications as well [8–10].

A majority of the applications and characterizations of CDs require them in solution form. It has been reported that the solvent environment, particularly solvent polarity, strongly influences the emission intensity and wavelength of CDs [11–13]. In addition to solvent effects, the concentration of CDs in solution plays a decisive role in determining their optical characteristics. Typically, increased concentration leads to emission quenching and bathochromic shift in the emission spectrum, often attributed to Forster resonant energy transfer (FRET) and π - π stacking interactions [14,15]. Though the photoluminescence (PL) quenching mechanisms mentioned above have been widely investigated, photon reabsorption serves as a significant yet underexplored mechanism that can give rise to PL quenching in CDs. Reabsorption arises due to the overlap between the absorption and emission spectra of a material. In CDs, this spectral overlap is substantial, making reabsorption a key factor in their optical behavior. Photon reabsorption in CDs has been reported earlier, wherein one subset of dots emits higher energy photons that are subsequently absorbed by another subset of dots, which eventually re-emit at a lower energy [16,17]. This reabsorption and re-emission processes have been explored in the modulation of the fluorescent color of CDs [18]. However, for dots where the absorption–emission overlap occurs in the tail region of the absorption spectrum, the re-emission process becomes insignificant.

In this study, we systematically investigated the role of photon reabsorption in the emission spectrum of CDs by focusing on the wavelength-dependent absorption of emitted photons. Reabsorption effects in the PL spectrum were examined and isolated by performing excitation depth-dependent photoluminescence measurements, where the path length of emitted photons within the carbon dot solution was varied. This measurement provided insights into how reabsorption impacts both the overall PL intensity and the PL peak position. The observed decrease in the net PL intensity with a gradual redshift in the PL peak position at higher concentrations of dots and larger path lengths signified the role of reabsorption. Time-resolved photoluminescence study was carried out at different concentrations to rule out aggregation effects.

The CDs were synthesized using the solvothermal method [19]. 2.0 gram citric acid was dissolved in the mixture of ethanol (25 ml) and formamide (25 ml) by ultrasonication for 10 min at room temperature. The resultant transparent solution was transferred to a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 125 °C for 5 h. A magenta colored solution was obtained which was cooled down to room temperature. Later, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant solution was then filtered using a
0 . 22
μ m
filter attached to a syringe to remove larger-sized particles. From the finally obtained parent CD solution, having concentration
C
0
, further concentrations were sequentially made by diluting in ethanol and used for optical measurements. Dropcasted films of CD solution on cleaned glass substrates were used for FTIR, Raman and XRD measurements.

The absorption and emission spectra of CD solutions were measured using Jasco V-570 spectrophotometer and Jasco FP-6600 spectrofluorimeter, respectively. The structure and size distribution of the carbon dots were obtained from HR-TEM measurements done using Jeol 3010 HR-TEM setup, keeping the acceleration voltage at 200 kV to avoid sample damage. FTIR measurement was done using Bruker VERTEX 70 spectrometer. The Raman and XRD spectra were acquired by Horiba-JobinYvon (HR 800 UV) and PANalytical Aeries benchtop X-ray diffractometer, respectively. Transient photoluminescence measurements were done using the Horiba Deltaflex TCSPC system with 405 nm laser excitation.

Photon reabsorption occurs if there is an overlap of emission and absorption spectra of an optically active material. As the emission from the sample, typically in solution form, passes through the mass of the same solution, a fraction of the emission gets absorbed. The resultant emission spectra get modified according to the wavelength dependence of the absorption coefficient of the emitter. It is assumed that the solvent has no absorption or emission in the wavelength range of interest. The strength of re-absorption is proportional to the path length traversed by the emitted light through the solution and the concentration of the solute (sample). Hence, the effect of re-absorption can be isolated and measured for a given solution concentration by measuring the PL spectra as a function of the path length traversed by the emitted beam through the solution.

In this work, we studied the re-absorption process in CD solution by varying the path length of the emitted light inside the cuvette, as shown in Fig. 1, while keeping all other parameters unchanged. The carbon dot solution of fixed concentration (fraction of
C
0
) was taken in 1 cm × 1 cm × 4.5 cm quartz cuvette. The cuvette was mounted on a translation stage such that the depth at which the excitation beam was focused could be varied. A 405 nm cw-laser was used as the excitation source and the emitted light from the sample was dispersed using a monochromator and collected in a photo-multiplier tube. We used phase-sensitive detection of the signal using a lock-in amplifier for a better signal-to-noise ratio. The focus of the laser was fixed in position while the cuvette was translated, as shown in Fig. 1. The depth (d) was defined as the distance of the excitation spot within the solution, from the edge of the cuvette facing the monochromator. Depth (d) is same as the path length traversed by the emitted light inside the sample. As the cuvette was translated towards the monochromator, d increased. Thus, the amount of reabsorption of the emitted light can be varied with d.

HR-TEM image was obtained to confirm the formation of CDs (Supplementary Fig. 1). The image shows the distribution of CDs with varying sizes and clearly visible lattice fringes. The sizes of the CDs varied from 1.5–4 nm with most of the CDs having their diameters in the range from 2–3.5 nm. Raman and XRD measurements were done for the drop-casted carbon dot films to understand the structure and crystallinity of the CDs. The Raman spectrum (Supplementary Fig. 2a) exhibited characteristic D and G bands [19]. Similarly, the XRD spectrum (Supplementary Fig. 2b) indicated a fairly crystalline carbon core with graphitic structure within the CDs [20].

The absorption spectrum of the carbon dot solution (Fig. 2) had two peaks. The sharp peak centered around 230 nm corresponded to the
π –
π
∗
transition arising from the carbon core [6]. The broader peak centered around 340 nm (n-
π
∗
) was associated to the oxygen related surface functional groups [2,5]. The FTIR spectrum (Supplementary Fig. 3) gives further details of the surface functional groups present in the CDs, including carboxyl, carbonyl and amine groups [19,21]. The CDs showed broad emission ranging from 400 nm to 600 nm for 390 nm excitation (Fig. 2). The broad emission arose due to the presence of surface states whose energies lay within the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels [2,22]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration dependent PL quenching in CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
PL spectra of carbon dot solutions were measured for different concentrations (fractions of
C
0
) of dots in ethanol. Initially, the PL intensity increased gradually with the concentration (Fig. 3a). This increase in emission was due to the increase in the number of dots. Further increase in the carbon dot concentration caused the PL intensity to saturate and decrease eventually. Fig. 3b shows the non-monotonous behavior of the PL peak intensity with the increase in concentration.

Concentration-induced luminescence quenching in carbon dots (CDs) has been previously reported [23], typically attributed to aggregation effects such as Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and
π – π
stacking interactions. Interestingly, aggregation can also modulate photoluminescence through electron redistribution and suppression of
π – π
interactions leading to a bathochromic shift in the emission spectra [23,24].

Aggregation effects are also associated with changes in the PL lifetime of the carriers [25,26]. Our measurement showed a negligible spectral shift in the emission peak across varying CD concentrations, with the individual spectra residing within the same emission envelope (Fig. 3a).

To rule out the presence of aggregation effects, the emission lifetime of CDs was also measured at two different concentrations and wavelengths. For 405 nm excitation, the carbon dot showed bi-exponential decay at 445 nm and 525 nm (Fig. 4). The time constants obtained from the fitting (Table 1) showed negligible change with the increase in concentration. Thus, it can be concluded that, for our CD solutions, the origin of the PL quenching was not due to aggregation effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reabsorption in the emission spectra of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of photon re-absorption is considerable when there is an overlap between the absorption and emission spectra of the sample, such that, when the emitted beam passes through the solution it gets partially reabsorbed. Then, the measured emission intensity can be considered to be the resultant of two competing mechanisms (i) radiative recombination of the excitons generated by the absorption of photons and (ii) the reabsorption of the emitted photons as the beam passes through the sample. While the first process enhances the PL signal, the second results in attenuation of the emitted light, particularly at wavelengths with higher absorption coefficients. Both the absorption of excitation light and the reabsorption of emitted photons increase with CD concentration, thereby influencing the net emission intensity observed. To model this behavior in a qualitative manner, we fitted the PL peak intensity as a function of concentration (Fig. 3b), using the expression given in Eq. (1).
(1)
I
(
λ
e x c
,
λ
e m
, C )
=
I
0
( 1 −
e
−
A
e x c
C
)
(
e
−
A
e m
C
)

Here, I is the observed emission intensity at a given excitation wavelength
λ
e x c
and emission wavelength
λ
e m
, and C is the concentration of CDs.
I
0
denotes the excitation intensity. The first term in the bracket,
1 −
e
−
A
e x c
C
represents the fraction of excitation light absorbed by the CD solution. This term increases with the concentration C , and is proportional to the absorption coefficient,
α
e x c
, of CDs at the excitation wavelength. The parameter
A
e x c
corresponds to the product of
α
e x c
and the path length traveled by the excitation light, which remains constant during the concentration-dependent PL measurement. The second term,
e
−
A
e m
C
, accounts for the transmittance of the emitted light as it propagates through the solution. This term reflects the exponential attenuation of emitted photons due to reabsorption, which becomes more significant with increasing concentration. Here,
A
e m
is the product of the absorption coefficient of CDs at the emission wavelength
(
λ
e m
)
and the path length traversed by the emitted light, which does not vary in the concentration-dependent PL measurement. The concentration of dots is the sole variable influencing the fit. The fitting matched fairly with the experimentally obtained data (Fig. 3b). This primarily hinted at photon reabsorption as the mechanism responsible for the PL quenching in CD solution for the given concentration regime.

To further elucidate the effect of reabsorption in the emission spectrum of CD solution, using the experimental technique proposed in Section 2.3, we measured excitation depth (d) dependent PL spectra for CD solution for two different concentrations. Fig. 5 shows the measured PL spectra for CD solutions of concentrations
0 . 0066
C
0
and
0 . 143
C
0
at different depths (d) of excitation. A 435 nm long pass filter was used to avoid the excitation light entering the monochromator, which resulted in the partial cut off of the PL signal at wavelengths below 435 nm in Fig. 5.

The PL spectrum for the CDs showed a peak at 445 nm for excitation at 405 nm. The PL intensity decreased with an increase in depth of excitation without any observable change in the emission spectra for low concentrations of CDs (Fig. 5a). For higher concentrations of CDs, as shown in Fig. 5b, the reduction in the PL intensity is more substantial with the change in depth. The spectrum showed an apparent redshift in the peak position with the increase in depth. To understand the reason for the redshift, we compared the decrease in PL intensities for different emission wavelengths (Fig. 6).

From Fig. 6, it can be seen that the quenching of emission is substantial for shorter wavelengths compared to longer wavelengths. It is this non-uniformity in the extent of quenching that manifests as a redshift in the PL spectra with the increase in depth. These observations can be explained on the basis of photon re-absorption in the solution, which depends on the absorption coefficient of the solution and the path length traversed by the emitted light in the sample medium. As a control experiment, we performed measurements on coumarin dye diluted in ethanol where re-absorption is expected to be negligible. The measured spectra for coumarin showed no shift or PL quenching with depth of excitation (Supplementary Fig. 4).

The measured PL intensities of CD solution for 450 nm, 475 nm, and 500 nm, as a function of depth of excitation were fitted with single exponential functions of the form
e
− α C d
(Supplementary Fig. 5). The fitting for change in PL intensity with depth at 450 nm is given as inset in Fig. 6. The product of absorption coefficient with concentration (
α
1
C
) obtained from the fitting are given in Table 2. The values of the product of absorption coefficient and concentration (
α
2
C
) obtained from the UV–vis experiment bears a good resemblance with the values obtained from the fitting. This implied that the effect of varying excitation depth on the intensity of emitted light is analogous to the Beer–Lambert effect in an UV–vis absorption measurement conducted in transmission mode, hence confirming the reabsorption effect.

To account for the redshift in the PL peak, arising from the non-uniformity in the extend of quenching, the PL spectrum at 0 mm depth excitation was divided by the absorbance in that wavelength regime. As shown in the normalized plots in Fig. 7, the division generates a spectrum which nearly replicates the measured PL spectrum at 7 mm depth. The spectral shift was illustrated by assimilating the optical absorption in the overlapping region as shown in Fig. 7. This is a clear indication of photon-reabsorption in our sample.

We identified photon reabsorption as the primary cause of PL quenching in CD solution and investigated its effects on the PL spectra. The CDs exhibited concentration quenching in solution, with no change in the shape or shift in the emission peak of the PL spectra as concentration varied. Moreover, the lifetime of the CDs remained unchanged with increasing concentration, confirming the absence of aggregation effects. Hence, we looked into photon reabsorption which can also lead to PL quenching. We proposed an excitation depth-dependent PL measurement technique that can isolate the changes in the PL spectrum of CDs due to reabsorption. The changes in the PL spectrum with varying excitation depth showed that the photon reabsorption is significant at higher concentrations of dots and the effect scales up exponentially with the path length traveled by the emitted light in the sample. A gradual and apparent redshift was observed in the PL peak position of the dots with an increase in the path length. The observed redshift in the peak position has been corrected by considering the contribution from the tail of optical absorption, which overlaps with the emission spectrum and gives rise to reabsorption in CDs.

Our findings suggested that photon reabsorption profoundly impacts the emission spectra of CDs in solution. This effect needs to be considered since the spectral content of the emitted light is crucial in applications such as bioimaging or sensors, where CD solutions are used. The depth-dependent PL measurement can serve as a tool to extract the imprints of reabsorption in the PL spectrum of any other emitting species with considerable absorption–emission overlap.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:

MMC S1
